agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Evolution of the Shipment aggregate from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental change to the core domain model of a delivery platform, affecting the Shipment aggregate root boundary, all downstream consumers, partner integrations, and 2 million existing production records. This is not a simple schema migration — it redefines what a Shipment *is*.",
    "bounded_context": "Shipment / Delivery Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shipment", "definition": "A unit of goods being transported from an origin to one or more delivery destinations. The aggregate root." },
      { "term": "Recipient", "definition": "A party designated to receive part or all of a Shipment at a specific address, identified by name, address, phone number, and delivery preferences." },
      { "term": "Delivery Preference", "definition": "Recipient-specific instructions governing how and when a delivery should occur (e.g., time window, signature required, leave at door, safe place instructions)." },
      { "term": "Primary Recipient", "definition": "In a multi-recipient shipment, the recipient designated as the default contact and billing reference. Required for backward compatibility and operational clarity." },
      { "term": "Split Shipment", "definition": "A shipment with multiple recipients where goods are divided and routed to different addresses. Distinct from a multi-drop route where the same driver visits multiple stops." },
      { "term": "Partner Integration", "definition": "External API consumers that receive shipment data in the current single-recipient format and must be migrated or adapted." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient at all times.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A shipment with zero recipients has no destination and cannot exist in a physically coherent state. This is a data integrity constraint — the system is corrupt if violated.",
      "testable_assertion": "Any attempt to create a Shipment with zero Recipients, or to remove the last Recipient from a Shipment, MUST fail with a validation error."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Each Recipient within a Shipment MUST have a name, address, and phone number.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "These are the minimum fields required to physically deliver goods and contact the recipient. Without any one of these, a delivery attempt cannot be made.",
      "testable_assertion": "Creating or updating a Recipient with a null/empty name, address, or phone number MUST be rejected."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Each Recipient within a Shipment MUST have an associated set of delivery preferences, which defaults to standard delivery if not explicitly specified.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Delivery preferences are a business policy enhancement. The system can function with sensible defaults; a VP could change what the default preferences are.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a Recipient is added without explicit delivery preferences, the system assigns default delivery preferences. The Recipient entity always exposes a non-null delivery preferences object."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A multi-recipient Shipment MUST designate exactly one Primary Recipient.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Operational processes (billing, escalation contact, return-to-sender, partner API responses) require a single authoritative recipient. Without this, downstream systems and human operators cannot resolve ambiguity.",
      "testable_assertion": "A Shipment with more than one Recipient MUST have exactly one marked as primary. Removing the primary Recipient MUST either promote another or fail. A single-recipient Shipment implicitly designates that Recipient as primary."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "All 2 million existing single-recipient Shipments MUST be representable in the new model without data loss or behavioral change.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Data integrity across migration is non-negotiable. Existing shipments are contractual records. Any loss or mutation of production data corrupts the system of record.",
      "testable_assertion": "After migration, every existing Shipment has exactly one Recipient marked as primary, with identical name, address, and phone values as the pre-migration record. No shipment loses its recipient data."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Recipient addresses within the same Shipment MAY be identical (e.g., same address, different contact persons).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a valid business scenario (e.g., two people at the same office). Enforcing uniqueness would be an artificial constraint not grounded in physical reality.",
      "testable_assertion": "A Shipment with two Recipients sharing the same address but different names/phones is accepted without error."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Partner APIs currently receiving single-recipient payloads MUST continue to receive a valid single-recipient representation until explicitly migrated to the multi-recipient format.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a compatibility policy, not a data integrity constraint. The business could choose to force-migrate partners on a timeline.",
      "testable_assertion": "Partner API responses for a multi-recipient Shipment return the Primary Recipient in the existing single-recipient field structure. No partner integration breaks when a Shipment gains multiple recipients."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The delivery status of a Shipment with multiple Recipients is determined by the aggregate status of all individual Recipient deliveries.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A Shipment cannot be marked 'delivered' if one recipient has not received their portion. The shipment state must reflect physical reality across all destinations.",
      "testable_assertion": "A Shipment with 3 Recipients where 2 are delivered and 1 is pending MUST NOT have an overall status of 'Delivered'. It should reflect a partial or in-progress state."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A new Shipment is being created",
      "when": "The request includes zero Recipients",
      "then": "The system rejects the request with a validation error indicating at least one Recipient is required"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A new Shipment is being created",
      "when": "The request includes three Recipients with distinct addresses and delivery preferences",
      "then": "The Shipment is created with all three Recipients persisted, each retaining their individual delivery preferences, and one is designated as Primary"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "An existing single-recipient Shipment in production",
      "when": "The data migration runs",
      "then": "The Shipment retains its original Recipient with identical name, address, and phone, that Recipient is marked as Primary, and default delivery preferences are assigned"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A multi-recipient Shipment exists",
      "when": "A partner API requests the Shipment data using the legacy format",
      "then": "The API returns the Primary Recipient in the existing single-recipient payload structure with no schema-breaking changes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A multi-recipient Shipment with 3 Recipients",
      "when": "2 Recipients have been delivered and 1 is still pending",
      "then": "The Shipment overall status reflects partial delivery, not 'Delivered'"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A multi-recipient Shipment",
      "when": "A user attempts to remove the last remaining Recipient",
      "then": "The system rejects the removal and the Shipment retains at least one Recipient"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A multi-recipient Shipment where the Primary Recipient is being removed",
      "when": "Other Recipients still exist on the Shipment",
      "then": "The system either requires a new Primary to be designated in the same operation or automatically promotes the next Recipient, ensuring the Primary invariant is never violated"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A Shipment with one Recipient",
      "when": "A second Recipient is added via the API",
      "then": "The original Recipient remains Primary, the new Recipient is added as non-primary, and both have independently queryable delivery preferences"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A Shipment is created with 100+ Recipients", "expected_behavior": "The system should enforce a reasonable upper bound on Recipients per Shipment (business policy, not invariant). Excessively large recipient lists likely indicate a bulk mailing scenario that should use a different domain concept (e.g., Distribution or Campaign)." },
    { "scenario": "Two Recipients on the same Shipment have identical name, address, and phone", "expected_behavior": "The system should accept this (could be intentional — e.g., two packages to the same person) but may surface a warning. Recipients should remain distinguishable by their own identity/ID, not by their field values." },
    { "scenario": "A Recipient's address is updated after the Shipment is in-transit", "expected_behavior": "Address changes on in-transit shipments must follow existing redirection business rules. For multi-recipient shipments, only the specific Recipient's delivery is affected — not the entire Shipment route." },
    { "scenario": "Partner API receives a webhook for a multi-recipient Shipment event", "expected_behavior": "The webhook payload must be backward-compatible. The Primary Recipient populates the legacy single-recipient fields. A new optional field exposes the full recipient list for partners who have opted into the v2 schema." },
    { "scenario": "A Shipment transitions from single-recipient to multi-recipient after invoicing has occurred", "expected_behavior": "Adding a Recipient post-invoicing has financial implications. The system must either trigger a re-invoicing event or block the addition depending on the Shipment's lifecycle stage. This needs explicit business policy definition." },
    { "scenario": "All Recipients on a multi-recipient Shipment refuse delivery", "expected_behavior": "The return-to-sender process must handle the case where there is no single 'refused by' party. The Shipment should aggregate refusal reasons per Recipient and follow the return policy keyed to the Primary Recipient's origin." },
    { "scenario": "Concurrent updates: two users add/remove Recipients on the same Shipment simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "Optimistic concurrency must prevent violating the at-least-one-Recipient invariant. A race condition that results in zero Recipients is a system corruption event." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Multi-recipient shipments likely increase average order value and enable new product offerings (gift-to-multiple, corporate distribution). However, pricing models must evolve — is a 3-recipient shipment priced as one shipment or three? This is an unresolved business question with significant revenue impact. Migration costs include engineering effort, testing, and partner coordination.",
    "operational": "Significant operational complexity increase. Dispatchers must now reason about multi-stop routes. Delivery status tracking becomes more nuanced (partial delivery states). Customer support must handle per-recipient issues on a single shipment. Sorting and warehouse processes may need to split physical packages per recipient.",
    "user": "Positive for end-users who want to send to multiple recipients in one transaction. However, UX complexity increases — tracking pages, notification emails, and delivery confirmations must now handle N recipients clearly. Confusion risk if users don't understand Primary Recipient designation.",
    "risk": "HIGH. This is a foundational model change affecting the core aggregate. Risks include: (1) Data migration failure on 2M records, (2) Partner API breakage despite backward-compatibility efforts, (3) Subtle bugs in status aggregation logic, (4) Performance degradation from 1:N joins on hot paths, (5) Domain drift if 'multi-recipient shipment' is conflated with 'multi-drop route' — these are distinct logistics concepts.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End Customers", "impact": "New capability to send to multiple recipients. Must be educated on how tracking and notifications work per-recipient." },
      { "group": "Delivery Drivers / Couriers", "impact": "Route planning and delivery confirmation processes change. A single shipment may now require multiple stops. Driver apps must surface per-recipient instructions." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Increased complexity in handling inquiries. Must be able to look up and resolve issues per-recipient within a shipment." },
      { "group": "External Partner APIs", "impact": "Must maintain backward compatibility. Partners need migration path to v2 schema. Breaking changes could violate SLAs." },
      { "group": "Finance / Billing", "impact": "Pricing model must account for multi-recipient shipments. Invoicing, refunds, and cost allocation per-recipient are new concerns." },
      { "group": "Warehouse / Fulfillment", "impact": "Physical package splitting and labeling processes may need to change if recipients require separate packages." },
      { "group": "Product / Analytics", "impact": "Reporting and KPIs built on 1:1 Shipment:Recipient assumption will break. Metrics like 'deliveries per shipment' gain new meaning." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is thin on several critical business questions: pricing model for multi-recipient, lifecycle constraints on when recipients can be added/removed, upper bounds, and the physical logistics distinction between multi-recipient (split packages) vs multi-drop (same package, multiple stops). Delivery preferences are mentioned but not defined. These ambiguities prevent confident acceptance criteria for the full feature scope.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "At-least-one-Recipient invariant is unambiguous and physically grounded",
      "Backward compatibility requirement for partner APIs is clear",
      "Data migration for existing 2M records must be lossless",
      "Primary Recipient concept is necessary for operational and integration continuity"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What 'delivery preferences' specifically includes — this term is undefined in the spec",
      "Whether multi-recipient means split physical packages or multi-stop single package",
      "Pricing and invoicing model for multi-recipient shipments",
      "Lifecycle rules — can recipients be added/removed at any shipment stage?",
      "Upper bound on recipients per shipment",
      "How shipment status aggregation should work across recipients (proposed BR-8 is my best interpretation)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The Primary Recipient concept is needed but was not explicitly stated in the spec — I'm introducing it based on operational necessity and partner compatibility requirements",
      "Delivery preferences default to a standard set if not specified — the spec doesn't define the default behavior",
      "Existing partner APIs must not break — inferred from the mention of external partners, not explicitly stated as a requirement",
      "Multi-recipient implies distinct physical deliveries, not just multiple contacts at one address"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'delivery preferences' without defining what this includes. Is it time windows? Signature requirements? Safe-place instructions? Authority-to-leave? Temperature controls? The scope of this concept drastically affects the domain model, UI, and driver workflow.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product Team", "Engineering", "Delivery Drivers", "End Customers"],
      "options": [
        "Define a minimal set: time window preference + delivery instruction text + signature required boolean",
        "Define a comprehensive set modeled on industry standards (time window, safe place, neighbor delivery, authority to leave, special handling)",
        "Make delivery preferences an extensible key-value model to avoid future schema changes"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Start with a concrete minimal set based on existing single-recipient delivery options the platform already supports, then extend. Extensible key-value models tend to erode domain meaning."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Is a multi-recipient Shipment a logical grouping (one package, multiple contact people) or a physical split (separate packages to separate addresses)? These are fundamentally different logistics operations with different cost structures, routing, and warehouse processes.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Warehouse / Fulfillment", "Delivery Drivers", "Finance / Billing", "Product Team"],
      "options": [
        "Multi-recipient = multiple physical deliveries from one order (split shipment)",
        "Multi-recipient = multiple contact persons at potentially different addresses, each receiving a portion",
        "Support both via a Shipment Type discriminator (single, multi-drop, split)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Clarify with product team immediately. If this is a split shipment, the Recipient should own a 'package manifest' or 'items' sub-collection. If it's multi-contact, the model is much simpler. Getting this wrong will require a costly re-architecture."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No pricing or invoicing model defined for multi-recipient shipments. Is a 3-recipient shipment billed as 1 shipment or 3 deliveries? Are there per-recipient surcharges? This has direct revenue impact and affects the Billing bounded context.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance / Billing", "End Customers", "Product Team"],
      "options": [
        "Price per shipment regardless of recipient count",
        "Price per recipient/delivery stop",
        "Base shipment price + per-additional-recipient surcharge"
      ],
      "recommendation": "This must be defined before implementation. The pricing model affects whether Recipient is just a delivery target or a billable unit, which changes the domain model."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No lifecycle constraints specified. Can a Recipient be added to a Shipment that is already in-transit? After delivery? After invoicing? The answer determines whether Recipients are mutable throughout the Shipment lifecycle or locked at certain stages.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Customer Support", "End Customers", "Finance / Billing"],
      "options": [
        "Recipients are locked once the Shipment leaves 'Created' status",
        "Recipients can be added/removed until the Shipment is in-transit, with restrictions after",
        "Recipients are always mutable but changes trigger re-routing and re-invoicing events"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Define explicit lifecycle stage gates for recipient mutability. Most logistics platforms lock recipient changes after dispatch, with a separate 'redirection' process for in-transit changes."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
